context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on the city case study data table for Urban Studies, and I’m a little unsure how to find and organize the required geographic and demographic data. Could you help me figure out what sources to use and how to structure it?
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Good starting point to think about sources early. Before I point you anywhere — what city did you choose, and have you already tried looking anything up? What kinds of sources do you typically go to when you need factual data about a place?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a broad orientation question. Rather than giving them a list of sources or a structure, I should use guided discovery to have them engage with what they already know. I'll acknowledge their question and ask them what they've already tried or what they think might be good starting points, keeping my response bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta, and I’ve only done a quick search so far. I’m not totally sure which sources count as reliable for things like GDP, race/ethnicity, and climate zone, so I’d like a little help sorting that out.
exchanges.tutor: Good choice — Jakarta is a rich case study. So, when you think about data like population or GDP, what kinds of organizations do you think would be most likely to collect and publish that kind of official information? What makes a source trustworthy for that type of data?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified their city and has a reasonable concern about source reliability. Rather than listing reliable sources for them, I should use guided discovery to have them think about what makes a source reliable for statistical/geographic data. This keeps them engaged in the process and builds research skills.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing government sources first, then maybe the World Bank or UN data? I think trustworthy means it says where the data came from and it’s not just some random website, but I’m a bit fuzzy on which source is best for each category.
exchanges.tutor: That's solid reasoning — primary sources and established international organizations are exactly the right instinct. Now try applying that logic: look at the required categories in the assignment (location, population, race/ethnicity, physical area, income per capita, GDP, Koppen climate zone). Which of those do you think would come from an Indonesian government source, and which might need an international one?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good instincts about source reliability. Rather than mapping out which source fits each category for them, I should affirm their reasoning and push them to apply it themselves to the specific data categories in the assignment.

exchanges.student: I think population, area, and maybe race/ethnicity would probably come from an Indonesian census or statistics office. GDP and income per capita might be from either Indonesia’s stats agency or World Bank, and Köppen climate zone feels like it might come from an academic or climate map source.
exchanges.tutor: That breakdown is spot-on. Now the next step is actually going and finding the numbers. Indonesia's statistics agency is called BPS (Badan Pusat Statistik) — worth checking out. Try pulling a few figures and come back with what you find. What's the first data point you'll look up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done a great job mapping sources to categories on their own. This is good progress. I should affirm this briefly and nudge them toward actually finding and using those sources, rather than continuing to discuss them abstractly. The conversation has covered source identification well enough — time to push toward action.

exchanges.student: I’d start with population since that seems easiest. I might be overthinking this, but if I find multiple population numbers from different years, should I just use the newest one, or should I try to match all the data to the same year?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to be asking. Think about it this way — why might it matter (or not) if your population figure is from 2020 and your GDP figure is from 2015? What could that inconsistency do to your analysis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a genuinely good methodological question. Rather than answering it directly, I can ask the student to think about why consistency might or might not matter for this assignment, guiding them to their own conclusion.

exchanges.student: I think it could make the city look richer or poorer per person than it really is, or just make the table kind of misleading. So I’m guessing I should match years as much as possible, but if I can’t, maybe note the year clearly for each number?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on both counts. Noting the year for each data point is good practice regardless. So — have you started filling anything into the table yet, or are you still in the research phase?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reasoned through this well and arrived at a sound methodological conclusion on their own. I should affirm briefly and move them forward toward actually building the table, since we've spent enough time on this conceptual point.

exchanges.student: Still mostly research phase. I found that Jakarta’s population depends on whether it means the province/capital region or the larger metro area, and now I’m a little stuck on which one the assignment probably wants.
exchanges.tutor: Good observation — that distinction matters. Think about the assignment's purpose: you're studying a city's climate vulnerabilities and where investment might be prioritized. Which geographic unit — the administrative city or the metro area — do you think is more relevant for that kind of analysis, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a real and meaningful methodological question, but the student can reason through it themselves by thinking about the assignment's purpose. I should ask a guiding question rather than resolving it for them.

exchanges.student: I want to say metro area because climate impacts and infrastructure kind of spill across boundaries, but now I’m second-guessing it because the assignment says “city” and the official data table might expect the administrative unit. I’m not sure how to choose without making it inconsistent.
exchanges.tutor: Both choices are defensible — the key is just being explicit about which unit you chose and why, right in your table. Make a decision, note it, and move on. Which will you go with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles a bit on this question. Both answers have merit and the resolution is simple: pick one and be transparent about it. I should be direct about this and push them to make a decision and move forward rather than dwelling further.

exchanges.student: Okay, then I’d go with the administrative unit for consistency and because it’ll be easier to source official data. I can add a note that the metro area is larger and relevant for some climate issues, if that makes sense.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sound choice and the note is a good addition. You've done solid thinking on methodology — now it's time to put it to work. Try filling in at least three or four rows of your table and share what you have. That'll give us something concrete to discuss.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a clear, well-reasoned decision and proposed a sensible way to handle the nuance. I should affirm briefly and push them to start actually filling in the table, since we're well into the conversation and they haven't produced any written work yet. Time to redirect toward output.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that makes sense. I’ll pull the administrative Jakarta data first and write the year/source in each row, then I can check if anything looks inconsistent after that.
exchanges.tutor: Good plan. Go ahead and do it — come back once you have a draft of the table and we can look at what you've put together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear plan and good methodology in mind. This is a good moment to encourage them to execute and come back with actual work. The conversation has been productive but it's time for the student to produce written output.
